narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and undergraduate GPA. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 42% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to help ensure students are set up for long-term success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing, signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong academic performance. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out as a strong positive influence, well above the average, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your undergraduate GPA, slightly below average, also helped by nudging things in a favorable direction, showing the model some reassurance in your prior college work. Less influential but still notable, your Year 1 decile in the lower ranks added a small push toward the failure prediction, while your lower-middle family income bracket worked slightly in your favor compared to typical applicants. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority and provided a minor positive boost. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having standout test scores that offset some concerns, but the model weighed your later-year school rankings most heavily as the key risk factor. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework, perhaps retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and consider programs offering bar prep support for students from similar backgrounds. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward with resilience. We wish you the best in your next steps.
